course_position.next_lecture_title: Poststrukturalisme, diskursiv psykologi og feministisk teori (Forelæsning 20, 2026-04-20)

summary_lines: Kilderne samler poststrukturalistiske perspektiver på etik, subjektivering og magt.
Foucault omformulerer etik som praksisser for selvforhold i historiske magtrelationer.
Davies viser, hvordan subjektet bliver til gennem diskurser og positionering i sociale praksisser.
key_points: Subjektivitet skabes gennem praksis og diskurs, ikke som en fast indre essens.
Selvomsorg er en relationel og politisk praksis, ikke privat selvoptimering.
Frihed udøves inden for magtrelationer gennem refleksiv transformation.
Etik handler om livsform og selvstyring i konkrete historiske kontekster.

sidecars.relative_path: Readings/W10L2 Sociokulturelle teorier - socialkonstruktionisme og poststrukturalisme/week.analysis.md
sidecars.content:
## Lecture frame
- Treat this lecture as a move away from personality as a stable inner essence and toward subjectivity as something formed in discourse, practice, and power relations.
- Keep the main lecture problem clear: how can psychology think about persons when selves are historically produced rather than simply discovered?
- Use the week to connect ethics, subject formation, and social construction rather than treating Foucault and Davies as unrelated authors.

## What to prioritize
- Make the poststructural shift explicit: the subject is produced through historically situated practices, norms, and available positions.
- Emphasize that both texts are about how persons become thinkable and governable, not only about "identity" in a loose everyday sense.
- Use the lecture theme to focus on what changes in psychology's understanding of freedom, agency, and selfhood when discourse and power become central.

## Relation between the readings
- Foucault gives the broader account of ethics, care of the self, and freedom within power relations.
- Davies makes the subjectivation point more concrete by showing how positioning in discourse shapes who one can be and how one can speak or act.
- The pair should sound cumulative: Foucault gives the conceptual horizon, Davies shows how this horizon works in lived social practice.

## Likely misunderstandings to avoid
- Do not reduce the week to "everything is language." The point is not linguistic idealism but historically organized practices of subject formation.
- Do not present Foucault's care of the self as private self-help. It is an ethical and political practice within relations of power.
- Do not treat agency as simple autonomy. Agency here is constrained, situated, and often takes the form of repositioning rather than sovereign choice.

readings.title: Davies (1990)
readings.subject_relative_path: Readings/W10L2 Sociokulturelle teorier - socialkonstruktionisme og poststrukturalisme/W10L2 Davies (1990).pdf

summary_lines: Davies develops a feminist poststructural account of the subject as discursively produced and positioned.
She shows how language and social practices open and constrain available identity positions.
Agency is treated as situated repositioning within, and sometimes against, normative discourse.
key_points: Subjects are constituted through discourse rather than pre-social interiors.
Power operates through ordinary meaning practices and category systems.
Positioning can be resisted, revised, or strategically inhabited.
Gendered subjectivity is continuously negotiated in interaction.

sidecars.relative_path: Readings/W10L2 Sociokulturelle teorier - socialkonstruktionisme og poststrukturalisme/W10L2 Davies (1990).analysis.md
sidecars.content:
## Text character
- This is a theoretical feminist poststructural text, not an empirical results paper.
- Read it as an argument about how subjects are constituted through discourse and positioning in social practice.

## What matters most
- Explain positioning carefully: the subject is made available through social categories, meanings, and interactional roles.
- Keep the relation between constraint and agency central. Davies is not denying agency; she is relocating it inside discursive and normative conditions.
- Show how ordinary language practices participate in making gendered subjectivity seem natural.

## Helpful framing
- The text is most useful when it clarifies how identity positions are socially offered, inhabited, resisted, and revised.
- Connect the argument to the broader lecture theme: personality and subjectivity are not pre-social interiors but historically and socially organized forms.

## Misreadings to avoid
- Do not flatten the text into a generic "social influence" claim. The stronger point is constitutive: discourse helps make the subject.
- Do not present discourse as merely words. It includes categories, norms, and socially available ways of being intelligible.
- Do not turn the text into pure determinism. Davies leaves room for repositioning and contestation.

readings.title: Foucault (1997)
readings.subject_relative_path: Readings/W10L2 Sociokulturelle teorier - socialkonstruktionisme og poststrukturalisme/W10L2 Foucault, M. (1997). s. 281-301 .pdf

summary_lines: Foucault reframes ethics as a practice of freedom rooted in the active care of the self.
Rather than universal moral law, he emphasizes historically situated practices through which subjects constitute themselves.
The interview links truth, power, and subjectivation to everyday ethical work on conduct.
key_points: Subjectivity is formed through practices, not simply discovered as an inner essence.
Care of the self is relational and political, not merely individual self-help.
Freedom is exercised within power relations through reflective transformation.
Ethics concerns style of life and practices of self-government.

sidecars.relative_path: Readings/W10L2 Sociokulturelle teorier - socialkonstruktionisme og poststrukturalisme/W10L2 Foucault, M. (1997). s. 281-301 .pdf.analysis.md
sidecars.content:
## Text character
- This is a conceptual interview text on ethics, freedom, and care of the self.
- Treat it as a clarification of Foucault's vocabulary and orientation, not as a conventional linear research article.

## What matters most
- Center the claim that ethics is a practice of self-formation within historically specific power relations.
- Explain care of the self as an active relation to oneself that is inseparable from broader truth practices and forms of conduct.
- Keep freedom and power together: freedom is exercised within power relations, not outside them.

## Helpful framing
- Use the text to show how subjectivity is something one does and is formed through, not something one simply uncovers as an inner core.
- Connect the text to the course theme that theories of personality also imply theories of normativity, governance, and possible transformation.

## Misreadings to avoid
- Do not describe the text as motivational self-improvement or private authenticity.
- Do not make ethics sound like obedience to universal rules; the emphasis is on practice, style of life, and self-relation.
- Do not present power as only external domination. The point is that subject formation happens within power-laden practices.

## Slide role
- Use the slides as a lecture map, not as a standalone source of claims.
- Let the slide sequence determine what the lecturer seems to treat as the main conceptual progression.

## What to prioritize from the deck
- Reconstruct the move from individualist or essentialist understandings of personality toward sociocultural and poststructural accounts of subject formation.
- Use the deck to foreground which distinctions the lecture itself prioritizes: discourse, positioning, ethics, freedom, power, and subjectivation.
- Where the slides compress too hard, expand only enough to recover the argumentative line rather than paraphrasing slide bullets one by one.

## Relation to the readings
- Let the slides help prioritize what is most important in Davies and Foucault, especially where the lecture is making a synthetic point across them.
- If the slide framing simplifies the texts, keep that visible rather than pretending the lecture and readings are identical.

## Misreadings to avoid
- Do not over-trust isolated slide slogans; reconstruct the implied lecture logic.
- Do not turn the lecture into a generic overview of social constructionism. Keep the emphasis on what this material changes in how psychology thinks about persons and selfhood.
